messages.content: I have multiple union statement. i wan to create single result cte from it.

You can use the UNION statement to combine multiple SELECT statements into a single result set. Here's an example:

WITH cte1 AS (
   SELECT column1, column2 FROM table1
   UNION
   SELECT column1, column2 FROM table2
),
cte2 AS (
   SELECT column3, column4 FROM table3
   UNION
   SELECT column3, column4 FROM table4
)
SELECT * FROM cte1
JOIN cte2 ON cte1.column2 = cte2.column3;

In this example, we have two CTEs (cte1 and cte2) that each use a UNION statement to combine data from multiple tables. We then join those two CTEs together to create a single result set. Note that the columns being joined together (column2 and column3) must have the same data type and be named the same in both CTEs.
create a single CTE from 5 union all statement

To create a single CTE from 5 UNION ALL statements, you can use the following format:

WITH combined_data AS (
    -- First SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table1
    UNION ALL
    -- Second SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table2
    UNION ALL
    -- Third SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table3
    UNION ALL
    -- Fourth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table4
    UNION ALL
    -- Fifth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table5
)
SELECT *
FROM combined_data;

In this example, we create a common table expression (CTE) named "combined_data" that uses UNION ALL to combine the SELECT statements from five different tables. Each SELECT statement selects three columns (column1, column2, column3) from its respective table. The CTE "combined_data" will hold all the rows from the five tables as a single result set. 

After the combined_data CTE is created, you can use it in subsequent SQL statements to perform additional operations and analysis on the combined data from all five of the tables.
To create a single CTE from 5 UNION ALL statements and then I want to get min and max

To get the minimum and maximum values from the single CTE created by combining 5 UNION ALL statements, you can use the following syntax:

WITH combined_data AS (
    -- First SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table1
    UNION ALL
    -- Second SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table2
    UNION ALL
    -- Third SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table3
    UNION ALL
    -- Fourth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table4
    UNION ALL
    -- Fifth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table5
)
SELECT MIN(column1) as min_val, MAX(column1) as max_val
FROM combined_data;

In this example, we have added an additional SELECT statement to the CTE "combined_data" to return the minimum and maximum values of the "column1" column in the combined data. We use the MIN() and MAX() functions to calculate these values. 

Finally, we select the calculated minimum and maximum values using the aliases "min_val" and "max_val". This will return a single result set with the minimum and maximum values of the "column1" column in the combined data from all five tables.
To create a single CTE from 5 UNION ALL statements and then left join that result with new table and then get the min max from overall joined data

To create a single CTE from 5 UNION ALL statements, left join that result with a new table, and then get the minimum and maximum values from the overall joined data, you can use the following code:

WITH combined_data AS (
    -- First SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table1
    UNION ALL
    -- Second SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table2
    UNION ALL
    -- Third SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table3
    UNION ALL
    -- Fourth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table4
    UNION ALL
    -- Fifth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table5
),
joined_data AS (
    SELECT cd.column1, cd.column2, cd.column3, jt.column4
    FROM combined_data cd
    LEFT JOIN join_table jt
    ON cd.column2 = jt.column2
)
SELECT MIN(column1) as min_val, MAX(column1) as max_val
FROM joined_data;

In this example, we have created a new CTE named "joined_data" that first left joins the CTE "combined_data" with a new table named "join_table". The join is based on the "column2" column in both tables.

The "joined_data" CTE then returns the columns "column1", "column2", "column3", and "column4" from the joined data. Finally, we use the MIN() and MAX() functions to get the minimum and maximum values of the "column1" column from the overall joined data that includes data from all five tables.

Note that the column names used in the left join and the select statement correspond with the names of the columns in the tables being joined. You may need to adjust the column names used in the join and select statements to match the column names in your own tables.
To create a single CTE from 5 UNION ALL statements, left join that result with a new table, and then get the columns from left joined table, minimum and maximum values from the overall joined data

To create a single CTE from 5 UNION ALL statements, left join that result with a new table, and then get the columns from the left joined table, the minimum, and maximum values from the overall joined data, you can use the following code:

WITH combined_data AS (
    -- First SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table1
    UNION ALL
    -- Second SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table2
    UNION ALL
    -- Third SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table3
    UNION ALL
    -- Fourth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table4
    UNION ALL
    -- Fifth SELECT statement
    SELECT column1, column2, column3
    FROM table5
),
joined_data AS (
    SELECT cd.column1, cd.column2, cd.column3, jt.column4
    FROM combined_data cd
    LEFT JOIN join_table jt
    ON cd.column2 = jt.column2
),
min_max_data AS (
    SELECT MIN(column1) as min_val, MAX(column1) as max_val
    FROM joined_data
)
SELECT joined_data.column1, joined_data.column2, joined_data.column3, joined_data.column4, min_max_data.min_val, min_max_data.max_val
FROM joined_data
CROSS JOIN min_max_data;

In this example, we have created a CTE "min_max_data", which uses the previously created "joined_data" CTE to get the minimum and maximum values of the "column1" column from the overall joined data that includes data from all five tables.

Finally, we select the columns "column1", "column2", "column3", "column4" from the "joined_data" CTE, along with the minimum and maximum values from the "min_max_data" CTE. We use the CROSS JOIN operator to combine the "joined_data" CTE with the "min_max_data" CTE to get a single result set that includes the desired columns. 

Note that the column names used in the join, select, and cross join statements correspond with the names of the columns in the tables being joined and selected. You may need to adjust the column names used in these statements to match the column names in your own tables.
I want to calculate average exception resolve time when an exception from to do status to resolve status in qliksense

To calculate the average exception resolve time when an exception moves from the "to do" status to the "resolve" status in QlikSense, you can use the following steps:

1. Identify the relevant tables in QlikSense that hold the necessary data. This will likely involve tables that track exception details (such as the exception description, the status of the exception, and when it was opened and resolved), as well as tables that contain information about the users involved in exception management.

2. Write a SQL query that joins these tables together and filters the data to include only exceptions that have moved from "to do" status to "resolve" status.

3. Use the DATEDIFF function to calculate the time between when the exception was opened and when it was resolved, expressed in the desired time units (e.g. seconds, minutes, hours, etc.).

4. Calculate the average time using the AVG function.

Here's some sample SQL code that demonstrates these steps:

SELECT AVG(DATEDIFF(second, open_time, resolve_time)) AS exception_resolve_time_avg
FROM exceptions_table et
INNER JOIN users_table ut1 ON et.assignee_id = ut1.user_id
INNER JOIN users_table ut2 ON et.resolver_id = ut2.user_id
WHERE et.status = 'resolve' AND et.previous_status = 'to do';

In this example, we have created a SELECT statement that calculates the average exception resolve time in seconds by joining the "exceptions_table" with the "users_table" twice (once to get the assignee information, and once to get the resolver information). We filter the data to include only exceptions that have moved from "to do" status to "resolve" status, and then use the DATEDIFF function to calculate the time between when the exception was opened and when it was resolved. We express this time difference in seconds, and then calculate the average using the AVG function.

calculate the average exception resolve day when an exception moves from the “to do” status to the “resolve” status in QlikSense. we just have the exception to do date and resolve date with no user id

To calculate the average exception resolve time in days when an exception moves from the "to do" status to the "resolve" status in QlikSense, and we only have the exception's to do date and resolve date with no user ID, you can use the DATEDIFF function in SQL to calculate the number of days it took to resolve the exception and then calculate the average. Here are the steps:

1. Identify the relevant tables that hold the necessary data, i.e., the exceptions table that includes the to do date and resolve date.

2. Write a SQL query that selects the relevant exception data and calculates the number of days it took to resolve each exception by subtracting the to do date from the resolve date using the DATEDIFF function.

3. Calculate the average of the resolved exceptions by using the AVG function.

Here's an example SQL statement:

SELECT AVG(DATEDIFF(day, to_do_date, resolve_date)) AS avg_resolve_day
FROM exceptions_table
WHERE status = 'resolve' AND previous_status = 'to do';

In this example, we create a SELECT statement that calculates the average number of days it took to resolve an exception by using the DATEDIFF function to calculate the number of days between the to do date and resolve date, and then taking the average of the resulting values with the AVG function. We then filter the exceptions to include only those that moved from "to do" status to "resolve" status. The resulting output will give you the average number of days it takes to resolve an exception.

To calculate the average exception resolve time in days when an exception moves from the “to do” status to the “resolve” status in QlikSense, and we only have the exception’s to do date and resolve date with no user ID, and we just have single column status and no previous status column

To calculate the average exception resolve time in days when an exception moves from the "to do" status to the "resolve" status in QlikSense, and we only have the exception's to do date and resolve date with no user ID, and we just have a single column 'status' and no 'previous status' column, you can modify the SQL query as follows:

SELECT AVG(DATEDIFF(day, to_do_date, resolve_date)) AS avg_resolve_day
FROM exceptions_table
WHERE status = 'resolve';

In this example, the SQL query is simplified since we do not have a 'previous status' column to filter by. The query still selects the relevant exception data from the 'exceptions_table' and calculates the number of days it took to resolve each exception by subtracting the to do date from the resolve date using the DATEDIFF function. We use the AVG function to calculate the average resolved time, and we filter the exceptions to include only those with a status of 'resolve'.

Note that this method assumes that all exceptions that have a status of 'resolve' have gone through the "to do" status before that. If there are any exceptions that have been resolved without going through the "to do" status, they will be included in the average.

To calculate the average exception resolve time in days when an exception moves from the “to do” status to the “resolve” status in QlikSense, and we only have the exception’s to do date and resolve date with no user ID, and we just have a single column ‘status’ and no ‘previous status’ column and then create a qliksense chart for this kpi

To calculate the average exception resolve time in days when an exception moves from the "to do" status to the "resolve" status in QlikSense, and we only have the exception's to do date and resolve date with no user ID, and we just have a single column 'status' and no 'previous status' column, you can use the following steps to create a QlikSense chart for this KPI:

1. Load the relevant data from the exceptions table that includes the to do date and resolve date into QlikSense.

2. Create a new sheet in QlikSense.

3. Add a KPI object from the visualization panel.

4. Configure the KPI object to display the average exception resolve time in days using the expression editor. The expression editor should contain the following expression:

=Avg(DateDiff('day', To_Do_Date, Resolve_Date))

This expression uses the DateDiff function to calculate the average number of days it takes to resolve an exception. 

5. Add any desired formatting, such as a custom title or number formatting. 

6. Save and publish the sheet to your QlikSense app.

The resulting KPI chart will display the average exception resolve time in days for exceptions that have gone through the "to do" status before being resolved. Note that the chart will show the average resolved time across all data, as there is no 'previous status' column to filter out data that might not be relevant.

To calculate the average exception resolve time in days when an exception moves from the “to do” status to the “resolve” status in QlikSense, and we only have the exception’s to do date and resolve date with no user ID, and we just have a single column ‘status’ and no ‘previous status’ column. how can I calculate this in qliksense no sql

In QlikSense, you can use an expression in a KPI object to calculate the average exception resolve time in days when an exception moves from the "to do" status to the "resolve" status, using the available data fields. Here are the steps:

1. Load the relevant data from the exceptions table containing the to do date and resolve date in QlikSense.

2. Create a new KPI object by opening a new sheet and clicking on the 'Create New Sheet Object' button at the bottom of the sheet.

3. In the 'New Sheet Object' dialog box that appears, select 'KPI' and click 'OK'.

4. In the 'KPI Properties' panel, give the KPI a title and subtitle.

5. In the 'Data' section, set the expression to:

    =Avg(Date#(Resolve_Date) - Date#(To_Do_Date), 'D')

This expression uses the Date# function to convert the strings representing the date values to date format, and then takes the average of the number of days between the resolve date and the to do date using the Avg function.

6. Set the number formatting for the KPI value to show the result in a readable format. You can do this by clicking the 'Number Formatting' tab and selecting 'Custom' as the format. Enter a number format that represents the format you would like, for example, "#,##0.##" to display the average as a number with two decimal points.

7. Save and exit from KPI properties panel.

8. Save and publish the sheet to your QlikSense app.

The resulting KPI will display the average exception resolve time in days using the available data fields. Note that this average will include all resolved exceptions, even if they have not been assigned to a specific user.
I want to know the average time of an exception from it's created date to done status in qliksense

To get the average time of an exception from its created date to done status in QlikSense, you can follow these steps:

1. Identify the relevant tables in QlikSense that hold the necessary data, i.e., the exceptions table that includes the created date and status of each exception.

2. Write a SQL query that selects all the relevant exception data, calculates the time required for each exception to move from the created status to the done status, and uses the AVG function to calculate the average time.

3. In QlikSense, create a KPI object that displays the average exception time calculated in the previous step.

Here is an example SQL query to calculate the average time of an exception from its created date to done status:

SELECT AVG(DATEDIFF(day, created_date, done_date)) AS average_exception_time
FROM exceptions_table
WHERE status = 'done';

In this example query, we have used the DATEDIFF function to calculate the difference in days between the created date and done date for all exceptions that are in the done status. We then use the AVG function to calculate the average time for all the done statuses.

Here are the steps to create a KPI object that displays the average time of an exception from its created date to done status:

1. In QlikSense, create a new sheet and click on the 'Create New Sheet Object' button at the bottom of the sheet.

2. In the 'New Sheet Object' dialog box that appears
